<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The top-level scope and `pkgver` in this PKGBUILD contain only variable assignments and a conditional dependency addition, with no command substitutions or function calls that would execute code during `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` or `makepkg --printsrcinfo`. The `source` array points to the official Ruffle GitHub repository via `git+https`, which is the expected upstream source. No malicious network behavior, obfuscation, or unexpected payloads are present. The duplicate source assignment and the use of a sha256sum on a VCS source are packaging hygiene issues, not security threats.
</details>
<summary>No malicious code executes during source fetch or PKGBUILD parsing.</summary>
</security_assessment>

The PKGBUILD sources Ruffle from the official GitHub repository using a specific nightly tag, fetches dependencies with `cargo fetch --locked` and `npm ci`, and builds Rust and web components normally. All package functions copy or install only the expected artifacts (binaries, desktop files, web apps, browser extensions) into `$pkgdir`. There are no obfuscated commands, no unexpected network requests beyond the declared upstream, and no system modifications outside the application scope. The duplicate `source` array assignment and the omitted `chromium-extension-ruffle.key` file (overwritten by the second assignment) are hygiene concerns but not malicious. The extension configuration for Chromium uses the standard Google update URL, which is normal.
</details>
<summary>Standard AUR PKGBUILD for Ruffle Nightly; no malicious behavior found.</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for builder.ts:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This is a standard TypeScript configuration module for the Ruffle Flash Player emulator. It translates user-provided configuration options (e.g., `BaseLoadOptions`) into calls on a `RuffleInstanceBuilder` object. All operations are local in-memory: setting properties like `allowScriptAccess`, `backgroundColor`, `quality`, `frameRate`, etc. The `parseColor` function parses a hex color string into an RGB integer; the `parseDuration` function converts a duration value to seconds; the `isMobileOrTablet` function checks `window.orientation` (a browser API). There are no network requests, no file system access, no obfuscated code, and no execution of externally fetched code. The file performs only expected application configuration and does not deviate from standard packaging or upstream source.
</details>
<summary>No malicious behavior; standard configuration module for Ruffle.</summary>
</security_assessment>

<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file is a standard Ruffle (Flash emulator) loader. It uses TypeScript imports and dynamic `import()` to load local modules (`../dist/ruffle_web`), fetches WebAssembly files from the same local directory (`../dist/`), and handles progress reporting. There are no external network requests to unexpected hosts, no obfuscated code, no suspicious system commands, and no deviation from normal packaging or application loading behavior. The use of feature detection (`wasm-feature-detect`) and conditional imports for WebAssembly extensions is appropriate. All operations serve the stated purpose of loading Ruffle from the package's own bundled assets.
</details>
<summary>
Standard Ruffle loader with local resources; no malicious behavior found.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This TypeScript file from the Ruffle project is a standard module for installing Ruffle into a web page. It registers a source API, sets up a public API on `window.RufflePlayer`, and optionally calls a plugin polyfill. No network requests, obfuscated code, dangerous commands (eval, base64, curl, wget), or unusual file operations are present. The code is consistent with the normal functionality of a Flash Player emulator and does not exhibit any signs of malicious behavior or supply-chain attack. The polyfill call is expected for plugin detection and is a normal part of this project's design.
</details>
<summary>Standard packaging code, no malicious behavior detected.</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This PKGBUILD is a standard template for a nightly binary package of the Ruffle Flash Player emulator. The source is fetched from the official GitHub releases (`https://github.com/ruffle-rs/ruffle/releases/download/`), which is the project's own upstream. The `package()` function only installs the binary, documentation, license, icon, desktop file, and metainfo file from the extracted tarball using standard `install` commands. There are no suspicious network requests, no obfuscated code, no eval or base64 usage, and no unexpected file operations. Checksums are present as placeholders (to be filled by the template processing), which is acceptable for a template. No malicious behavior is detected.
</details>
<summary>Standard PKGBUILD template, no malicious code found.</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for update-font.sh:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The script downloads two Google Noto fonts from the official GitHub repository (notofonts/notofonts.github.io) using pinned commit hashes, then processes them with standard fonttools utilities (`pyftsubset`, `pyftmerge`, `ttx`) to subset and modify the fonts for use with the Ruffle Flash emulator (as referenced in a related issue). The final gzip compression step is unconventional but not obfuscated or malicious; it appears to produce a raw gzip stream without metadata for the application's needs. There is no evidence of data exfiltration, backdoors, unexpected network requests, or other supply-chain attack patterns. The script operates entirely within the expected scope of preparing font assets for the ruffle package.
</details>
<summary>Harmless font processing script for Ruffle package.</summary>
</security_assessment>

Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/ruffle-nightly.git to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-3e7ipz5y...

Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines standard sources, including a Git repository from the official project (ruffle-rs/ruffle.git) using a tagged nightly release, and a local key file. The `sha256sums` array contains a valid checksum for the Git source, though it is redundant since VCS sources are not verified via checksum in practice—this is normal for AUR packages and not a risk.

Sourcing the PKGBUILD during `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` will execute top-level assignments and the `pkgver()` function if present, but no `pkgver()` function is defined here. The variables are simple strings or conditionals based on `_system_wasm_bindgen`, which is hard-coded `false`. There is no command substitution, dynamic code evaluation, or remote execution at the top level.

The `source` array points only to the legitimate, expected upstream repository. No suspicious downloads, obfuscated code, or data exfiltration occurs during source fetching or PKGBUILD sourcing. While the `prepare()` and `build()` functions install `wasm-bindgen-cli` via `cargo install`, those phases are skipped due to `--noprepare` and `--nobuild`, so they are out of scope for this narrow gate.

Thus, simply sourcing the PKGBUILD and fetching sources poses no security risk.
</details>
<summary>
No malicious code in sourcing or fetch stage
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- No malicious code in sourcing or fetch stage[/]

LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD is for the `ruffle-nightly` package, a well-known open-source Flash emulator. It fetches source code from the official GitHub repository (`ruffle-rs/ruffle.git`) using a tagged nightly release (e.g., `nightly-2026-06-19`), which is a standard and expected practice for -nightly packages. The source integrity is verified via a fixed `sha256sums` hash, ensuring reproducibility and protection against tampering.

During `prepare()`, `wasm-bindgen-cli` is conditionally installed if not using the system version. While `cargo install` is used, it installs a known version derived from `Cargo.lock` (`require_wasm_bindgen_version=$(tomlq ...)`), reducing risk. This is common in Rust-based AUR packages when system tools are unavailable. No network requests occur at build time beyond the initial git source fetch.

The build uses standard Rust and npm tooling (`cargo build`, `npm run build:repro`), and all installation steps copy built artifacts or static assets into standard system paths. Some packages provide browser extensions and web assets, which are installed under `/usr/share/webapps`, `/usr/lib/firefox`, or `/usr/share/chromium/extensions` — all standard locations. The Chromium extension metadata points to Google's official update URL, which is expected behavior.

There are no signs of obfuscated code, unauthorized network access, exfiltration, or execution of unverified remote code. The use of `jq`, `yq`, and `tomlq` is for standard configuration parsing. Although `source` appears duplicated (a typo), it does not affect functionality. Overall, the script follows AUR packaging best practices, and all operations are consistent with the stated purpose of building and installing Ruffle from its upstream source.
</details>
<summary>Safely builds Ruffle from official source</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for build.rs:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The `build.rs` file is a standard Rust build script used in Cargo-based projects to perform custom build steps. It invokes functions from a module `build_playerglobal` to generate AVM1 and AVM2 playerglobals using source files located in `src/avm1/globals/` and `src/avm2/globals/`, writing outputs into the Cargo-generated `OUT_DIR`. These generated files are then referenced via environment variables for use in the crate compilation.

The script only performs local filesystem operations, uses standard Cargo metadata instructions (`cargo:rerun-if-changed`, `cargo:rustc-env`), and does not execute external commands, download content, spawn processes, or access the network. It relies on compile-time features (e.g., `known_stubs`) and follows normal patterns for code generation in Rust crates. All paths are derived from the project directory and `OUT_DIR`, with no attempts to read sensitive files or exfiltrate data.

The behavior is consistent with legitimate build-time code generation for the Ruffle project, which emulates Flash runtime components. There is no evidence of malicious payload, obfuscation, or supply-chain compromise.
</details>
<summary>
No malicious behavior; standard Rust build script
</summary>
</security_assessment>

The script uses `utils` imports for storage, runtime, and option management, which are typical in WebExtensions.

There are no signs of malicious behavior. The use of `wrappedJSObject` is documented and necessary for Firefox extensions to interact with page scripts securely. No network requests to external domains are made directly in this file, and all URLs used are derived from `runtime.getURL`, which points to the extension's own resources.

While the script manipulates the DOM and uses postMessage for communication—which could be risky if misused—all such operations are scoped to the extension's functionality and include appropriate safeguards (e.g., checking message origin and ID). LLM auditresponse for load-ruffle.ts:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided JavaScript/TypeScript code is a loader module for Ruffle, a Flash Player emulator. It conditionally loads WebAssembly modules (`ruffle_web.wasm` or a fallback variant) based on detected WebAssembly feature support (e.g. SIMD, bulk memory). The code imports modules via static string literals (e.g., `import("../dist/ruffle_web")`), which is safe and standard in modern bundler environments. It uses `fetch()` to load a WebAssembly binary from a local relative URL, wrapped with optional download progress reporting through a `ReadableStream` — all client-side operations with no external network calls beyond the application's own assets.

There are no signs of malicious behavior: no data exfiltration, no dynamic code evaluation (e.g., `eval`, `Function`, base64 decoding of payloads), and no suspicious external requests. The placeholder `%FALLBACK_WASM%` is replaced at build time (as noted in the comment), which is a normal build-time substitution technique. The use of polyfills and feature detection is legitimate and scoped to ensuring compatibility. The code memoizes the Ruffle constructor to avoid repeated downloads, which is a reasonable performance optimization.

All operations are confined to loading and initializing the Ruffle runtime from the package’s own distribution files. This is consistent with expected behavior for a frontend asset loader in a WebAssembly-based application.
</details>
<summary>
Safe: No malicious code; standard asset loading
</summary>
</security_assessment>

<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided `background.ts` file is a background script for a Chrome/Edge extension, likely part of the Ruffle Flash emulator project. It implements standard browser extension APIs to conditionally register content scripts and declarative net request rules that redirect Flash (.swf) content to a built-in emulator page (`player.html`). The logic is focused on feature detection (e.g., `isHeaderConditionSupported`) and user preference management via `utils.getOptions()` and `storage.sync`. All operations—registering content scripts, setting up redirection rules for Flash files, and responding to messages—are legitimate extension behaviors and align with the expected functionality of a Flash compatibility layer.

There is no evidence of malicious behavior: no network requests to external domains, no obfuscated code, no file system or environment access, and no data exfiltration. The script interacts only with the browser's extension APIs (e.g., `chrome.runtime`, `chrome.declarativeNetRequest`, `chrome.scripting`) in ways consistent with the extension's purpose. The inclusion of specific sites like `4399.com` reflects targeted content script injection for compatibility, which is common in emulator or ad-blocking extensions. No dangerous commands or external payloads are involved.

The code uses standard patterns for service worker–based extensions, including top-level event listeners and asynchronous initialization. While it modifies network behavior, this is done transparently via the declared `declarativeNetRequest` API and only when user settings (`ruffleEnable`, `swfTakeover`) are active. These are expected capabilities granted to the extension by the user and declared in its manifest. No supply-chain or runtime attack vectors are present.
</details>
<summary>
Legitimate extension logic, no malicious code found
</summary>
</security_assessment>
